steps.title: Use the graph's shape
steps.content: The scatterplot is exponential and levels off near $y=18$ on the left. That means the model should include a vertical shift of $+18$, leaving those choices as the serious candidates.

steps.title: Check the scale at x = 0 and x = 3
steps.content: At $x=0$, $y=24(1.55)^{x}+18$ gives ${24}(1.55)^0+18=42$, but the graph is near $24$. The model $y=6(1.55)^{x}+18$ gives ${6}(1.55)^0+18=24$, matching the graph. At $x=3$, that model is also near $40$, which follows the plotted curve.

steps.title: Confirm against the choices
steps.content: $y=24(1.55)^{x}$ has no vertical shift, so it is too low on the left side. $y=24(1.55)^{x}+18$ has intercept $42$, too high at $x=0$. $y=(1.55)^{x}+23$ has the right low-end level but grows far too slowly; at $x=3$ it is only about $26.7$. $y=6(1.55)^{x}+18$ is near $40$ at $x=3$ and follows the plotted curve.
